
---
title: "Concert Operate for Batch Processing"
subtitle: "AI-powered event correlation and noise reduction for batch job failures"
format: html
---
```{=html}
<span class="dot">MD</span> &ensp;Mat Davis&ensp;
```
<br>
---
Description: "Concert Operate for Batch Processing"
---

## The Why

Operations teams managing large-scale batch workloads face a common and costly problem:
a single job failure produces a cascade of duplicate alerts and tickets, flooding incident
queues with noise and slowing response to genuine issues. Without a correlation and
deduplication layer, L1 operators spend significant time triaging tickets that represent
the same underlying event.

IBM Concert Operate addresses this by sitting between the event source and the ticketing
system, correlating, deduplicating, and suppressing noise before it becomes work.

### Problem Details

Batch job failures in enterprise environments are rarely isolated. A single failed job
triggers retries, dependency failures, and monitoring alerts, each potentially generating
its own incident ticket. At scale, this creates three compounding problems:

1. **Noise volume:** operators receive many tickets for what is functionally one incident,
   making it difficult to identify what actually requires human intervention.
2. **Cascade failures:** when one job fails and its downstream dependencies follow, each
   dependency generates independent alerts. Without cross-event correlation, the root cause
   is buried under consequential noise.
3. **Retry churn:** automated retry logic can generate repeated alert cycles before a job
   ultimately succeeds or fails definitively. Each retry cycle adds tickets with no
   incremental signal value.

The result is slower mean time to resolution (MTTR), higher operator fatigue, and reduced
confidence in the alert signal.

### Additional Context

The integration path for batch event ingestion depends on the existing operations toolchain.
In environments using a batch scheduler with an event forwarding agent (such as a Tivoli
`postemsg`-style mechanism), the near-term approach is to retarget that agent to deliver
events to Concert Operate rather than the legacy event aggregator. This requires no changes
to the batch scheduler itself. Only the outbound destination configuration changes.

A longer-term ingestion path using IBM Z Common Data Provider (CDP) for syslog-based
detection is available in environments with Concert for Z licensing and z/OS infrastructure.
